planting.note: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to sandy soil in full sun after last frost; blue porter weed blooms nearly year-round in frost-free climates and is one of the most important butterfly and hummingbird plants in the Gulf South.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to sandy soil in full sun; blue porter weed is a native Florida and Caribbean plant that produces long, slender spikes of tiny deep-blue to violet flowers that open a few at a time along the spike and are accessible to butterflies and hummingbirds that probe each floret in sequence — making it one of the most reliable butterfly attractors in Gulf Coast butterfly gardens, blooming nearly continuously through the warm season and year-round in frost-free zones. It naturalizes along roadsides and disturbed areas in south Florida. Plants are drought-tolerant once established and thrive in sandy, well-drained soil. Pinch growing tips to encourage bushier growth. Cut back hard if frost-damaged in zone 9.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in warm, moist sand or plant container specimens in well-drained, sandy to average soil in full sun after last frost; peregrina grows rapidly in warm climates and produces flowers almost continuously in frost-free zones.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, sandy to average soil in full sun in the warmest microclimates of zone 10–12; peregrina is a Cuban native with strikingly red star-shaped flowers borne in clusters nearly year-round, making it one of the most continuously blooming ornamentals in tropical and subtropical landscapes. Plants attract butterflies and hummingbirds avidly. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested — the seeds in particular are very poisonous and should not be accessible to children or pets. Drought-tolerant once established. In zone 9 container culture, bring indoors to a bright location before frost.

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost; red powderpuff grows rapidly and can reach 10–15 feet if unpruned. Prune after each bloom cycle to keep compact.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; red powderpuff is a showy South American shrub or small tree that produces spectacular globular flower heads made entirely of long, silky, deep-crimson stamens arranged in a perfect sphere 2–3 inches across, resembling a crimson powder puff — hence the common name. Flowers appear from fall through spring in zone 9–10 landscapes, often when little else is blooming. Hummingbirds and butterflies visit the flowers eagerly. Prune after each blooming cycle to control size and promote dense growth; unpruned plants can reach 15 feet. In zone 9, plant against a warm, south-facing wall and protect from frost. The related C. emarginata (Dwarf Pink Powderpuff) stays smaller and blooms nearly year-round.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container specimens in fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun after last frost; in zones below 9, grow in containers and bring indoors before frost.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; flowering maple is not a true maple but a tropical shrub with maple-like leaves and pendant, bell-shaped flowers in yellow, orange, red, pink, or white that hang in clusters from arching branches and bloom almost continuously in frost-free climates. An excellent hummingbird and butterfly plant. Pinch growing tips monthly to keep plants bushy and floriferous. Feed monthly with a balanced fertilizer during active growth. Container-grown plants can be overwintered indoors in a bright, cool room; reduce watering in winter and resume normal care in spring. Bring containers outdoors after last frost.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in warm, moist potting mix or plant container specimens near a strong trellis, fence, or arbor in full sun after last frost; blue sky vine grows aggressively and requires substantial support — it can cover a large pergola in a single season.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens against a strong pergola, fence, or arbor in full sun; blue sky vine is one of the most spectacular flowering vines for Gulf Coast landscapes, producing pendant clusters of enormous sky-blue trumpet flowers 3 inches across from summer through fall on vigorous vines that can grow 20–30 feet in a single season. The large, heart-shaped leaves and cascading flower clusters create an exotic tropical effect. In zone 9 plants die back after frost but regrow from the roots in spring; in zone 10–11 they are evergreen and bloom nearly year-round. Plants can become invasive in tropical areas; monitor spread. The species is sterile and does not set seed, so spreading occurs only by root suckering and vegetative growth.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container specimens in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun after last frost; Chinese hat plant requires warm temperatures and blooms most heavily in fall and winter in frost-free climates.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container specimens in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Chinese hat plant is a Himalayan shrub grown for its uniquely structured flowers — each blossom has an expanded, saucer-shaped orange-red calyx (the 'hat') surrounding a smaller tubular corolla, giving each flower the appearance of a Chinese mandarin's hat. The flowers appear in arching terminal clusters from fall through spring in frost-free climates when hummingbirds and sunbirds seek them out. The plant is drought-tolerant and blooms most prolifically during cooler, drier periods. Prune hard after bloom to rejuvenate and shape. In zone 9 protect from frost or grow in large containers.

planting.note: Plant divisions of the rhizomatous clump in spring after last frost in moist, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; musella is cold-hardy to zone 7 when mulched and re-sprouts from the underground rhizome after hard freezes kill the above-ground pseudostems.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun in spring; the dwarf Chinese yellow banana is a cold-hardier alternative to ornamental bananas, forming compact clumps of paddle-shaped, blue-green leaves and producing a spectacular, long-lasting, waxy yellow flower head resembling an enormous lotus blossom from atop the pseudostem after 2–3 years of growth. Unlike most bananas, the inflorescence can last for months rather than weeks, and the plant is monocarpic — the main stem dies after flowering but produces offset pups around the base that will flower in future years. Mulch the rhizome base heavily in zone 7 over winter; the pseudostems may freeze but the roots reliably re-sprout. Excellent focal-point specimen plant for tropical-themed gardens.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in warm, moist soil or plant container specimens in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun after last frost; alternanthera develops its best color in full sun and heat, and is perennial in zones 9–11.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun after last frost; alternanthera (Joseph's coat) is a tropical foliage plant grown for its intensely colored leaves in burgundy, purple, red, orange, or multicolored patterns that provide vivid, heat-resistant color throughout the summer and fall when many plants struggle. It is widely used in bedding schemes, tapestry plantings, and as an edging plant in formal gardens. Colors intensify with heat and full sun exposure. In zones 9–11 it is a perennial groundcover; elsewhere treat as an annual and either collect stem cuttings to overwinter indoors or replant from cuttings each spring. Shear periodically to maintain dense, compact form.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
